title: Memoize Decorator
description: A method decorator that caches synchronous method results using memoizee. Repeated calls with the same arguments return cached values; the cache is cleared automatically on ngOnDestroy to prevent memory leaks.

hints.title: Pure methods only
hints.content: Memoize caches by argument values. If the method also depends on changing this state (inputs, signals) that is not part of the args, results can become stale. Pass all relevant inputs as parameters.

hints.title: Object arguments use reference equality
hints.content: Two different object instances with identical content produce separate cache entries. Prefer primitive args or stable references.

hints.title: No module import needed
hints.content: Import Memoize directly from @acorex/core/memoize. The empty MemoizeModule is not required. Ensure experimentalDecorators: true is set in tsconfig.

hints.title: Lifecycle cleanup
hints.content: The decorator patches ngOnDestroy to call memoizedMethod.clear(). Implement ngOnDestroy on the class if you need custom teardown — the decorator chains your existing hook.

types.title: Memoize
types.description: Method decorator factory backed by memoizee.

methods.content:
/**
 * Wraps a class method with memoizee caching.
 * Cache key = method arguments (memoizee default resolver).
 * On ngOnDestroy, calls .clear() on the memoized function.
 *
 * @returns MethodDecorator
 */
Memoize(): MethodDecorator

content.data:
The @acorex/core/memoize package exports a single @Memoize() method decorator.

It is designed for expensive, deterministic class methods that are called repeatedly with the same arguments — for example formatting cell text in a large data table.

Under the hood it uses the memoizee library. The decorator:

• Replaces the method with a memoized wrapper
• Returns cached output when arguments match a previous call
• Patches ngOnDestroy to clear the cache when the instance is destroyed

sections.title: Basic Usage
sections.description: Apply the decorator to a class method.

sections.title: In a Service
sections.description: Memoize utility methods on injectable services.

sections.title: Pass All Inputs as Arguments
sections.description: Avoid stale cache when instance state matters.

content.data:
memoizee uses argument identity (SameValueZero for primitives):

• @Memoize() compute(5) and compute(5) → cache hit
• @Memoize() compute(5) and compute(5.0) → cache hit (SameValueZero)
• @Memoize() compute({ id: 1 }) twice with two different object literals → two cache entries
• @Memoize() compute('a', 'b') vs compute('b', 'a') → different entries (order matters)

For row objects in tables, the same row reference on re-render produces cache hits — which is usually desired during virtual scroll repaints.

sections.title: ngOnDestroy Chaining
sections.description: How cleanup interacts with your lifecycle hook.

content.data:
Use @Memoize() when:

• The method is synchronous and expensive (formatting, parsing, tree walks)
• Same arguments always produce the same result during the instance lifetime
• The method is called many times in templates or virtual scroll loops

Avoid @Memoize() when:

• The method has side effects (HTTP calls, DOM writes, state mutation)
• Results depend on time, randomness, or mutable this state not in args
• Arguments are always new object references (cache never hits)
• You need TTL or LRU eviction — memoizee keeps entries until clear()/destroy

For async HTTP caching, use RxJS shareReplay, Angular resources, or a dedicated cache service instead.

The @Memoize() decorator uses legacy TypeScript method decorators. The AcoreX workspace enables this in tsconfig.base.json:

experimentalDecorators: true

If you consume @acorex/core/memoize in an external project, ensure the same flag is set. Angular's signal-based APIs do not replace this decorator — it targets classic class methods specifically.
